metadata.title: HITRUST CSF v9.6
metadata.description: The HITRUST Common Security Framework v9.6 provides a comprehensive, certifiable framework for healthcare information security, combining HIPAA, ISO, NIST, and PCI requirements.

controls.title: Information Security Policy
controls.description: Establish and maintain an information security policy that governs the protection of healthcare information.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall establish, document, and maintain an information security policy approved by management that defines the approach to managing information security for protected health information and other sensitive data.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Review and approve the policy annually. Communicate to all employees and relevant third parties. Include scope, objectives, roles, and compliance obligations. Key questions: Is the security policy reviewed annually? How is policy communicated to staff? Evidence examples: Approved policy document, policy acknowledgement forms, review meeting minutes.

controls.title: Information Security Roles and Responsibilities
controls.description: Define and assign information security roles and responsibilities across the organization.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall define and assign information security roles and responsibilities to key personnel, including a designated information security officer responsible for oversight of the security program.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Designate a CISO or equivalent role. Define security responsibilities in job descriptions. Establish a security steering committee. Key questions: Is a security officer designated? Are security responsibilities documented in job descriptions? Evidence examples: Organization chart with security roles, job descriptions, committee charter.

controls.title: User Access Management
controls.description: Manage user access to healthcare information systems through formal provisioning and de-provisioning processes.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall implement formal user registration and de-registration procedures for granting and revoking access to healthcare information systems and protected health information.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Use unique user IDs for all users. Remove access immediately upon termination. Review access rights quarterly for clinical systems. Key questions: How are user accounts provisioned and de-provisioned? How often are access rights reviewed? Evidence examples: Account management procedures, access review reports, termination checklists.

controls.title: Privileged Access Management
controls.description: Control and monitor privileged access to healthcare systems containing protected health information.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall restrict and control the allocation and use of privileged access rights to healthcare information systems, including administrative and clinical administrator accounts.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Implement just-in-time privileged access. Monitor all privileged user activity. Require approval for privilege escalation. Key questions: Are privileged accounts managed with PAM? Is privileged activity monitored? Evidence examples: PAM configuration, privilege access requests, privileged session recordings.

controls.title: Access Control for Mobile and Remote Access
controls.description: Secure mobile and remote access to healthcare information and systems.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall implement secure access controls for mobile devices and remote access to healthcare information systems, including encryption, multi-factor authentication, and device management.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Require MFA for all remote access to PHI. Use VPN with strong encryption. Implement mobile device management for clinical devices. Key questions: Is MFA required for remote access? Are mobile devices managed centrally? Evidence examples: MDM policy, MFA configuration, remote access logs.

controls.title: Background Screening
controls.description: Conduct background screening for personnel with access to healthcare information.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall conduct background verification screening for all personnel with access to protected health information prior to employment, in accordance with applicable laws and regulations.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Screen based on role sensitivity including criminal history and professional references. Re-screen at appropriate intervals. Document screening results confidentially. Key questions: What background checks are performed? Are healthcare role-specific checks conducted? Evidence examples: Background check policy, screening records, role sensitivity classifications.

controls.title: Security Awareness Training
controls.description: Provide security awareness training covering healthcare-specific privacy and security obligations.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall provide security awareness and training programs to ensure all personnel understand their information security and privacy responsibilities, including HIPAA and HITECH obligations.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Conduct annual HIPAA privacy and security training. Include phishing awareness. Tailor training for clinical staff handling PHI. Key questions: Is HIPAA training conducted annually? How is training effectiveness measured? Evidence examples: Training records, completion certificates, phishing simulation results.

controls.title: Physical Access Controls
controls.description: Restrict physical access to facilities housing healthcare information systems.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall implement physical security controls to prevent unauthorized physical access, damage, and interference to facilities housing healthcare information systems and protected health information.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Secure server rooms and clinical areas with electronic access control. Maintain visitor logs. Use video surveillance at access points. Key questions: What physical controls protect healthcare data? How are visitors managed in clinical areas? Evidence examples: Physical access policy, access log audits, surveillance records.

controls.title: Workstation Security
controls.description: Secure workstations and devices that access protected health information.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall implement controls to secure workstations and devices that access, process, or store protected health information, including automatic timeouts and screen locks.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Enable automatic screen lock after 15 minutes of inactivity. Position screens to prevent shoulder surfing. Prohibit unauthorized software installation. Key questions: What workstation security controls are in place? Is automatic locking enabled? Evidence examples: Workstation security policy, screen lock configuration, workstation audits.

controls.title: Network Segmentation
controls.description: Segment networks to protect healthcare information systems from unauthorized access.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall segment networks to isolate healthcare information systems, clinical devices, and systems containing protected health information from untrusted networks.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Implement VLAN segmentation between clinical and corporate networks. Use firewalls to control inter-segment traffic. Isolate medical devices on separate network segments. Key questions: Is the clinical network segmented? How are medical devices isolated? Evidence examples: Network architecture diagram, firewall rule sets, segmentation test results.

controls.title: Network Monitoring
controls.description: Monitor networks for unauthorized access and anomalous activity.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall monitor networks for unauthorized access, anomalous activity, and potential security incidents affecting healthcare information systems.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Deploy network intrusion detection systems. Monitor for data exfiltration attempts. Correlate network alerts with healthcare system logs. Key questions: Are network monitoring tools deployed? How are anomalies escalated? Evidence examples: NIDS configuration, monitoring dashboards, incident escalation procedures.

controls.title: Incident Response Capability
controls.description: Establish incident response capabilities for healthcare security and privacy incidents.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall establish incident management capabilities to detect, respond to, and recover from information security and privacy incidents involving protected health information.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Develop incident response procedures covering breach notification per HIPAA requirements. Test response plans semiannually. Coordinate with legal and compliance teams. Key questions: Is there an incident response plan covering PHI breaches? How often is it tested? Evidence examples: Incident response plan, breach notification procedures, test exercise reports.

controls.title: Breach Notification
controls.description: Notify affected individuals and regulators in accordance with breach notification requirements.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall notify affected individuals, regulators, and other stakeholders in accordance with applicable breach notification laws and regulations, including HIPAA breach notification requirements.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Notify affected individuals without unreasonable delay and within 60 days. Notify HHS for breaches affecting 500 or more individuals. Maintain breach log. Key questions: What are breach notification timelines? How are breaches reported to regulators? Evidence examples: Breach notification procedures, notification letters, HHS breach reports.

controls.title: Business Continuity Planning for Healthcare
controls.description: Ensure continuity of healthcare services and protection of health information during disruptions.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall develop and maintain business continuity plans that ensure the continued availability of healthcare services and protection of health information during and after disruptions.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Conduct business impact analysis for clinical systems. Define RTO and RPO for patient care systems. Test plans annually including clinical scenarios. Key questions: Are BCPs in place for clinical systems? What are RTO/RPO for patient data? Evidence examples: BIA reports, BCP documents, continuity test results.

controls.title: Data Backup and Restoration
controls.description: Back up healthcare data and test restoration procedures regularly.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall back up protected health information and healthcare system data regularly, and test restoration procedures to ensure data can be recovered within required timeframes.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Perform daily incremental and weekly full backups. Test restoration quarterly. Store backups offsite encrypted. Key questions: How often are backups performed? Are restoration tests conducted? Evidence examples: Backup schedules, restoration test reports, offsite backup inventory.

controls.title: Regulatory Compliance
controls.description: Identify and comply with applicable healthcare privacy and security regulations.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall identify and comply with all applicable legal, regulatory, and contractual requirements related to healthcare information security and privacy, including HIPAA, HITECH, and state privacy laws.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Maintain a compliance register of applicable laws and regulations. Conduct compliance reviews annually. Update policies when regulations change. Key questions: How are regulatory changes tracked? How often is compliance assessed? Evidence examples: Compliance register, regulatory tracking processes, compliance assessment reports.

controls.title: Internal Audit
controls.description: Conduct internal audits to verify compliance with security policies and regulatory requirements.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall conduct internal audits at planned intervals to verify compliance with information security policies, procedures, and regulatory requirements for healthcare information protection.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Develop an annual audit plan covering all healthcare systems. Use independent auditors. Report findings to management with remediation plans. Key questions: Is an audit plan in place? Are audit findings tracked to closure? Evidence examples: Audit plan, audit reports, remediation tracking.

controls.title: Data Classification and Handling
controls.description: Classify and handle healthcare data according to its sensitivity and regulatory requirements.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall classify data according to its sensitivity, including protected health information, and implement appropriate handling controls based on classification levels.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Define classification levels including PHI, PII, and confidential business data. Label data with classification metadata. Apply controls based on classification. Key questions: Is data classification defined? How is PHI identified and labeled? Evidence examples: Data classification policy, labeling procedures, classification audit reports.

controls.title: Encryption of PHI
controls.description: Encrypt protected health information at rest and in transit to prevent unauthorized access.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall encrypt protected health information when stored and during transmission over public or untrusted networks, using approved cryptographic algorithms and key management practices.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Use AES-256 for data at rest. Encrypt all ePHI in transit with TLS 1.3. Manage keys using a centralized KMS. Key questions: Is PHI encrypted at rest? Is encryption used for all PHI transmissions? Evidence examples: Encryption policy, key management procedures, encryption verification reports.

controls.title: Data Retention and Disposal
controls.description: Retain and dispose of healthcare data in accordance with legal and regulatory requirements.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall retain healthcare data in accordance with legal and regulatory requirements and securely dispose of data when no longer needed, using methods that prevent reconstruction.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Follow HIPAA record retention requirements of minimum 6 years. Use secure destruction methods for paper and electronic media. Obtain certificates of destruction. Key questions: What are data retention periods? Are destruction methods certified? Evidence examples: Retention schedule, destruction policies, disposal certificates.

controls.title: Notice of Privacy Practices
controls.description: Provide notice of privacy practices to patients and handle requests for PHI access.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall provide a notice of privacy practices to individuals describing how their protected health information may be used and disclosed, and their rights regarding their health information.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Post the notice of privacy practices prominently. Provide notice at first service encounter. Update notice when practices change. Key questions: Is a notice of privacy practices provided? How are individuals informed of their rights? Evidence examples: Notice of privacy practices document, posting locations, distribution records.

controls.title: Individual Rights
controls.description: Support individual rights to access, amend, and request accounting of disclosures of their health information.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall establish procedures to support individual rights to access, request amendment of, and receive an accounting of disclosures of their protected health information.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Respond to access requests within 30 days. Provide accounting of disclosures for the past 6 years. Document all requests and responses. Key questions: How are individual rights requests handled? What are the response timelines? Evidence examples: Rights request procedures, request tracking logs, response records.

controls.title: Third Party Risk Management
controls.description: Assess and monitor third parties that access or process protected health information.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall assess and monitor the security and privacy practices of third parties and business associates that access, process, or store protected health information.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Execute business associate agreements with all third parties handling PHI. Conduct initial and annual risk assessments of business associates. Review independent audit reports. Key questions: Are business associate agreements in place? How are third parties monitored? Evidence examples: BAA agreements, vendor assessment reports, SOC 2 reports.

controls.title: Third Party Agreements
controls.description: Include security and privacy requirements in contracts with third parties handling healthcare data.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall include information security and privacy requirements in contractual agreements with third parties, including business associate agreements as required by HIPAA.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Define security requirements, breach notification obligations, and audit rights in contracts. Review contracts at renewal. Maintain a contract inventory. Key questions: Do contracts include security and privacy requirements? Are BAAs in place for all PHI disclosures? Evidence examples: Contract templates, signed BAAs, contract review records.

controls.title: Mobile Device Management
controls.description: Manage and secure mobile devices that access, process, or store healthcare data.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall implement mobile device management controls to secure mobile devices that access, process, or store protected health information, including encryption, remote wipe, and device passcode requirements.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Deploy MDM solution for all mobile devices accessing organizational data. Enforce device encryption and screen locks. Enable remote wipe capability. Key questions: Is MDM deployed? Can devices be remotely wiped? Evidence examples: MDM policy, device inventory, remote wipe test results.

controls.title: BYOD Policy
controls.description: Define and enforce a policy for bring-your-own-device access to healthcare information.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall define and enforce a bring-your-own-device policy that addresses security controls, acceptable use, and data segregation for personally owned devices accessing healthcare information.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Require containerization or workspace separation for corporate data. Prohibit rooted or jailbroken devices. Enforce minimum security baselines. Key questions: Is there a BYOD policy? How is corporate data segregated on personal devices? Evidence examples: BYOD policy, containerization configuration, device compliance reports.

controls.title: Risk Assessment for Healthcare
controls.description: Conduct risk assessments addressing healthcare-specific threats and vulnerabilities.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall conduct comprehensive risk assessments that identify, analyze, and evaluate risks to the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of protected health information.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Conduct risk assessments annually and when environmental changes occur. Address both security and privacy risks. Include all systems that store, process, or transmit PHI. Key questions: How often are risk assessments conducted? Do assessments cover all PHI systems? Evidence examples: Risk assessment methodology, assessment reports, risk register.

controls.title: Risk Mitigation
controls.description: Implement risk mitigation plans to address identified risks to healthcare information.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall develop and implement risk mitigation plans that prioritize and address identified risks to the security and privacy of protected health information.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Assign risk owners and remediation timelines. Track mitigation activities in a risk register. Report risk status to management quarterly. Key questions: Are risk treatment plans documented? How is remediation progress tracked? Evidence examples: Risk treatment plans, risk register updates, management review reports.

controls.title: Vulnerability Scanning for Healthcare Systems
controls.description: Conduct regular vulnerability scanning of systems that process protected health information.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall conduct regular vulnerability scanning of all systems and applications that process, store, or transmit protected health information to identify and remediate security weaknesses.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Scan clinical systems weekly for critical vulnerabilities. Use credentialed scanning where feasible. Remediate critical vulnerabilities within 15 days. Key questions: How often are healthcare systems scanned? What are remediation SLAs? Evidence examples: Vulnerability scan reports, remediation tracking, vulnerability management procedures.

controls.title: Patch Management
controls.description: Apply security patches to healthcare systems in a timely manner to address known vulnerabilities.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization shall establish a patch management process to ensure security patches are evaluated, tested, and deployed in a timely manner to systems handling protected health information.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Test patches in a non-production environment before deployment. Prioritize patches based on CVSS scores. Document any patch deferrals. Key questions: How are patches tested before deployment? What is the patch deployment timeline? Evidence examples: Patch management policy, patch deployment records, exception requests.
